sections.heading: How Compose Layouts Work
sections.content: Compose measures and places UI elements through a single-pass layout system that is fundamentally different from the View system's multi-pass measurement. Every composable is given a set of constraints by its parent — minimum and maximum width and height — and must measure itself and its children within those constraints, then report its own size back to the parent. Because each node is measured only once, Compose's layout system is inherently efficient and avoids the exponential slowdown that deeply nested Views could cause in the old system. Understanding constraints, alignment, and arrangement is the key to building precise, responsive layouts in Compose.

sections.heading: Layout System Fundamentals
items: Constraints — every composable receives min/max width and height from its parent; it must fit within these bounds
Measure — a composable measures all its children first, then determines its own size
Place — after measuring, the parent places each child at a specific offset within its bounds
Single-pass — each child is measured exactly once — no double measurement like the old View system
Intrinsic measurements — use IntrinsicSize.Min and IntrinsicSize.Max when you need to size based on children's natural sizes before placing
Tight constraints — parent forces an exact size on the child (min == max); child cannot choose its own size
Loose constraints — parent gives a range; child can choose any size within that range
Unbounded constraints — parent imposes no maximum (e.g., inside a ScrollColumn); child can be as large as it wants

rows.description: Arranges children vertically one after another
Arranges children horizontally one after another
Stacks children on top of each other; last child is on top
Vertically scrollable list that only composes visible items — equivalent to RecyclerView
Horizontally scrollable list that only composes visible items
Vertically scrollable grid of items — fixed column count or adaptive sizing
Pinterest-style staggered grid with items of varying heights
Wraps children to the next line when they overflow — like CSS flexbox wrap
Wraps children to the next column when they overflow vertically
Positions children relative to each other using constraints — available via separate dependency
rows.key_params: constraintSet or inline createRefs() and constrainAs()

sections.heading: Column and Row — Arrangement and Alignment
sections.content: Column and Row are the workhorses of Compose layouts. Column arranges children vertically and Row arranges them horizontally. Both accept an arrangement parameter that controls how children are distributed along the main axis (vertical for Column, horizontal for Row) and an alignment parameter that controls how children are positioned along the cross axis. Getting these two parameters right is the key to achieving most common layout patterns without needing a more complex layout composable.

rows.description: Places children at the top or start with no extra space — default for Column and Row
Places children at the bottom or end of the container
Centers all children in the middle of the main axis
Equal space between children; no space at the edges
Equal space around each child — half space at edges, full space between
Equal space between and around all children — equal at edges and between
Fixed gap of specified dp between each child — most commonly used arrangement
Centers each child horizontally within the Column
Aligns each child to the start edge of the Column
Aligns each child to the end edge of the Column
Centers each child vertically within the Row — most commonly needed Row alignment
Aligns each child to the top of the Row
Aligns each child to the bottom of the Row
Centers the Box's content both horizontally and vertically

sections.heading: The Modifier System — Deep Dive
sections.content: Modifiers are the primary mechanism for controlling a composable's size, appearance, behavior, and interaction. They form an ordered chain of transformations — each modifier wraps around the composable and the modifiers that come after it. This means modifier order is critical: Modifier.padding(16.dp).clickable { } is different from Modifier.clickable { }.padding(16.dp) because padding and click target area are determined by position in the chain. Every composable should accept a Modifier parameter with a default of Modifier to allow callers to customize it.

sections.heading: Size Modifiers
items: Modifier.fillMaxSize() — expands to fill all available space (both width and height)
Modifier.fillMaxWidth() — expands to fill the full available width
Modifier.fillMaxHeight() — expands to fill the full available height
Modifier.fillMaxWidth(0.5f) — fills 50% of the available width — fraction parameter (0.0 to 1.0)
Modifier.size(dp) — sets exact square size
Modifier.size(width = dp, height = dp) — sets exact width and height independently
Modifier.width(dp) — sets exact width; height determined by content
Modifier.height(dp) — sets exact height; width determined by content
Modifier.widthIn(min = dp, max = dp) — constrains width to a range
Modifier.heightIn(min = dp, max = dp) — constrains height to a range
Modifier.wrapContentSize() — sizes the composable to exactly fit its content
Modifier.defaultMinSize(minWidth, minHeight) — sets minimum size without overriding larger constraints

rows.description: Adds equal padding on all four sides
Sets horizontal (start+end) and vertical (top+bottom) padding separately
Sets each side independently using named parameters
Applies padding from a PaddingValues object — used with Scaffold's content padding
Inserts vertical empty space between composables in a Column
Inserts horizontal empty space between composables in a Row
Flexible spacer that pushes adjacent items to opposite ends of a Row or Column

sections.heading: Visual and Background Modifiers
items: Modifier.background(color) — fills composable area with a solid color
Modifier.background(color, shape = RoundedCornerShape(8.dp)) — solid color with rounded corners
Modifier.background(brush) — fills with a gradient: Brush.horizontalGradient(listOf(Color.Red, Color.Blue))
Modifier.clip(shape) — clips the composable's content to the given shape
Modifier.clip(CircleShape) — circular clip — use for profile photos
Modifier.clip(RoundedCornerShape(8.dp)) — rounded corners clip
Modifier.border(width, color, shape) — draws a border; shape must match clip shape for correct appearance
Modifier.shadow(elevation, shape) — draws a Material elevation shadow
Modifier.alpha(fraction) — sets opacity from 0.0 (invisible) to 1.0 (fully visible)
Modifier.graphicsLayer { rotationZ = 45f } — applies 2D/3D graphics transformations (rotation, scale, translation)

rows.description: Makes the composable respond to click events; shows ripple effect by default
Clickable with enabled/disabled state control
Supports single click, long click, and double click
For mutually exclusive selection — radio button behavior
For toggle on/off behavior — checkbox-like
Low-level scroll behavior — prefer verticalScroll() or horizontalScroll()
Makes a Column scrollable vertically — use rememberScrollState()
Makes a Row scrollable horizontally
Makes the composable focusable for keyboard and accessibility navigation
Adds accessibility semantics — role, content description, state descriptions

sections.heading: Weight Modifier in Row and Column
sections.content: The weight modifier is a scope-specific modifier available only inside Row and Column. It distributes remaining space proportionally among weighted children after unweighted children have been measured and placed. A child with weight(2f) receives twice the remaining space as a child with weight(1f). This is the Compose equivalent of layout_weight in LinearLayout and is essential for building flexible, proportional layouts.

sections.heading: weight Modifier Patterns
items: Row { Text("Label", Modifier.weight(1f)); TextField(Modifier.weight(2f)) } — label takes 1/3, field takes 2/3
Column { Header(); Content(Modifier.weight(1f)); Footer() } — header and footer are fixed; content fills remaining space
Equal distribution: Row { items.forEach { Text(it, Modifier.weight(1f)) } } — all items equal width
weight(fill = false) — the composable is weighted but not forced to fill its allotted space
weight only works directly inside RowScope and ColumnScope — not in Box or nested layouts
Weighted children are measured after all non-weighted children — non-weighted items get their natural size first
Combining weight with fillMaxWidth causes an exception — weight already controls the size; do not combine
Use Spacer(Modifier.weight(1f)) to push remaining items to one end of a Row or Column

rows.description: Renders each item in a list
Renders list with stable keys — enables smooth add/remove animations and correct scroll preservation
Renders each item with its index
Renders a single item — for headers, footers, or separators
Renders a header that sticks to the top as the user scrolls past its section
Adds padding inside the scroll container without clipping scroll content at the edges
Adds equal spacing between each list item
Remembers scroll position; use to programmatically scroll or read scroll offset

sections.heading: Box — Overlapping and Z-Ordering
items: Box stacks children on top of each other — last child in the lambda renders on top
contentAlignment = Alignment.Center — centers all children by default
Alignment.TopStart, TopCenter, TopEnd, CenterStart, Center, CenterEnd, BottomStart, BottomCenter, BottomEnd — all nine positions available
Modifier.align(Alignment.X) inside Box — overrides contentAlignment for a specific child
Use Box for overlay patterns: loading spinner over content, badge over icon, floating button over list
Example: Box { AsyncImage(...); if (isLoading) CircularProgressIndicator(Modifier.align(Center)) }
Box is not scrollable — wrap it in ScrollState if content may exceed bounds
For stacking with explicit Z ordering: use Modifier.zIndex(float) to control draw order

rows.description: Top app bar — typically TopAppBar or CenterAlignedTopAppBar
Bottom navigation or action bar
FAB positioned at the bottom-end corner by default
Host for displaying Snackbars — use SnackbarHostState for programmatic control
Main content lambda receives PaddingValues — MUST apply to content modifier

sections.heading: Custom Layout with Layout Composable
items: Use the Layout composable for custom measurement and placement that Column, Row, and Box cannot achieve
Layout(content = { children composables }) { measurables, constraints -> measurePolicy }
Measure each child: val placeables = measurables.map { it.measure(constraints) }
Calculate your own size from the children's measured sizes
layout(width, height) { placeables.forEach { it.placeRelative(x, y) } } — place each child at its position
placeRelative() respects RTL layout direction; place() uses absolute coordinates
Use SubcomposeLayout for cases where the measurement of one child depends on the measured size of another
Custom layouts are rare — most UI can be built with Column, Row, Box, and the weight modifier

rows.mistake: Nesting multiple scrollable containers (Column inside LazyColumn)
rows.fix: Never put a verticalScroll Column inside a LazyColumn — they conflict; use LazyColumn items() for all content

rows.mistake: Using fillMaxSize() on an item inside LazyColumn
rows.fix: LazyColumn has infinite height — use fillMaxWidth() on items; fillMaxSize() causes a crash

rows.mistake: Not applying Scaffold's padding to the content
rows.fix: Always apply PaddingValues from Scaffold's content lambda: Modifier.padding(paddingValues) or content is hidden behind bars

rows.mistake: Using hardcoded pixel values instead of dp
rows.fix: Always use dp for layout dimensions — pixel values are density-dependent and break on different screen densities

rows.mistake: Not passing Modifier as a parameter in custom composables
rows.fix: Always accept a modifier: Modifier = Modifier parameter in custom composables to allow callers to customize layout

rows.mistake: Using weight modifier outside Row or Column
rows.fix: weight is a scope-specific modifier — it only works as a direct child of Row or Column

rows.mistake: Forgetting key parameter in LazyColumn items
rows.fix: Always provide key = { item.id } for list items — enables correct animations and preserves scroll state on list updates

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Compose's layout system is built on a powerful but simple constraint-based model where each composable is measured once and placed by its parent. Column, Row, and Box cover the vast majority of layout needs — with Arrangement and Alignment parameters handling distribution and positioning, the weight modifier for proportional sizing, and Modifier chains for all visual styling, size, padding, and interaction behavior. LazyColumn and LazyRow efficiently handle large lists with their DSL-based item APIs. Scaffold provides the standard Material screen structure. For the rare cases where these primitives are insufficient, the Layout composable offers full control over measurement and placement.
